name: Avenc d'en Corbera
starting_point: Jardín de Flores urbanization (Ses Rotgetes de Canet), km 1 of MA-120 road between Esporlas and MA-1130 Palma-Valldemossa road
clothing: Coverall essential due to heavy mud
equipment: Vertical progression equipment, 20m and 35m ropes, 2 protectors, 5 carabiners. Expansion bolts are usually already installed.
description: A popular cave near Esporles, frequently used for caving courses due to its easy access. It begins with an 18m practice shaft, then descends through a second pit with two rigging points and an aerial 10m descent. The Great Hall leads via a crawl to the 75m Sala des Fang (Mud Hall). A 7m rope climb accesses a 30m upper gallery with formations. The deepest point (-41m) is in the Guano Halls, where bat colonies have declined.
full_description:
Near the town of Esporles is this interesting cavity, very frequently visited mainly due to its easy access as well as its technical characteristics, since it begins with a shaft of approximately 18 m that has served on many occasions for practice during the various caving courses that have been held on the island.

Once at the bottom, we will approach the first landing through a considerable slope, and after a downclimb of approximately a couple of meters, we will reach the second landing, from which we will descend to the second shaft of the day, where we will need to set up a couple of rebelays, performing an aerial descent of about ten meters after the second one.

Once in the Great Hall, we will advance to the back of it, looking for the crawl of about five meters through a gentle slope that will allow us to access the second hall called Sala des Fang (Mud Hall), of large dimensions, approximately 75 m. As we move through it, we will have to walk very carefully, watching not to step into any of the trenches found in the floor. On the right side of this hall, between two columns, we can find a tied rope (on the day of our visit, the rope was cut about 3 m from the ground) that will allow us to access the upper gallery after overcoming about seven meters of height. The Upper Gallery, about thirty meters long and of quite uneven width, is mainly concretioned in its final section.

Once the Upper Gallery has been finished and photographed, we must return again to the Sala des Fang to continue our visit. We will head to the back of it to begin ascending a steep slope covered with stalagmites and impressive columns, where the only care we need to take is not to slip since the floor is covered in mud. Once on the other side of the columns, and at the end of the slope, we can access, if we wish, some small chambers after crossing a sea of blocks.

On the way back to the entrance hall, we can visit the Guano Halls, named for the large amount of guano that can still be found in this place, although at present everything seems to indicate that the number of bats has declined — specifically, we could only observe one pair. Climbing first through the cone and then descending steeply to the deepest part of the cavity at -41 m, we will pass through a kind of arch that marks the beginning of the Guano Halls. I began the tour on the left side, climbing through the chaos of blocks. First, we find a small room also on the left side, lacking interest. We continue climbing while multiple concretions begin to appear, notable among them some beautiful parietal flowstones found in the upper part of the cave, which rises to about a meter and a half from the surface. The descent can be made by the same route or by looking for other alternatives.

For further reading:

Daniel Mayoral, Tomeu Mateu. MALLORCA BEAUTY IN THE DARKNESS. Published by Espeleo Mallorca SC 2006.
